text: The surgeon opens the retroperitoneal space between the round ligament and the enfandi, preparing to access the uterine arteries for control during the laparoscopic myomectomy.
The surgeon navigates along the external iliac vein, tracing its path to locate the omnicar artery deep within the retroperitoneal space.

The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery, applying traction to clearly visualize it. The artery is followed posteriorly and medially, revealing the first artery branching medially from the umbilical artery.
The surgeon successfully isolates and achieves control of the uterine artery during the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.
The uterine artery is clearly visible in the surgical field, highlighted against the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses two tracts on the umbilical artery to locate the uterine artery, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon develops the pararectal fossa to visualize the ureter, which is positioned medial to the uterine artery. A clip is placed at the origin of the uterine artery for secure occlusion.
The surgeon observes the pulsating uterine artery, with the superficial uterine vein visible just below it.
The surgeon controls the left uterine artery and proceeds to perform the same procedure on the right uterine artery, ensuring symmetrical management of both vessels.

The surgeon tracks the infundibulopelvic ligament medially, while an assistant uses scissors to coagulate the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament. The surgical team attempts to access deeper structures within the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers scissors near the external iliac vein, avoiding damage to the vein and any visible varicosities in the area.
The surgeon coagulates small bleeding vessels within the retroperitoneal space to ensure a clear view of anatomical landmarks.

The surgeon identifies the internal iliac artery and develops the space between the ureter, visible medially, and the uterine artery. The assistant retracts the uterine artery externally to facilitate the development of the pararectal space.
The surgeon visually identifies the bifurcation between the umbilical artery and the uterine artery within the surgical field.
The uterus is clearly visible along with the uterine artery. Both paramedical structures are seen, and a surgical clip is applied to the uterine artery.
The surgeon prepares to control a large pedunculated myoma, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation and section energy to efficiently remove a large pedunculated myoma, minimizing blood loss during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the myomectomy, with the uterine arteries controlled, ensuring minimal use of bipolar energy.
The surgeon performs hemostasis on the myoma bed using a 14mm one-vial stitch, securing the tissue to control bleeding.
The surgeon applies intracorporeal stitches to secure the surgical site, ensuring hemostasis in the myoma bed following the removal of the pedunculated myoma.
The assistants apply traction to both edges of the uterine scar, exposing the area for the surgeon to take a substantial amount of myometrial tissue to ensure effective hemostasis.
The surgeon performs an intracorporeal knotting technique using a double flat knot, visibly achieving partial hemostasis in the myoma bed.
The surgeon begins closure of the uterine scar by tying a knot at the medial part, using intracorporeal knotting techniques to achieve hemostasis.
The surgeon ties two or three knots on the uterine scar, ensuring adequate hemostasis by securing the myometrial tissue.
The surgeon places two additional stitches in the surgical site, using an intracorporeal knotting technique to enhance hemostasis.
The surgeon secures hemostasis by suturing the anterior part of the scar, followed by the posterior part, ensuring effective closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon inserts the needle into the second posterior part of the scar, preparing to place stitches for hemostasis.
The surgeon invaginates the myometrium into the myoma bed, using a technique aimed at reducing the risk of intramyometrial hematoma.
The surgeon ties a flat knot in the surgical site, ensuring it is well secured in the correct direction for optimal tension and stability.
The surgical field shows effective hemostasis, with no visible bleeding in the area surrounding the myoma bed.
The video shows the ischemic area surrounding the myometrial scar, indicating reduced blood flow in the tissue adjacent to the scar.
The surgeon places the final stitch in the surgical field, completing the suturing process.
The surgeon carefully places a stitch, ensuring that the proximal part of the fallopian tube is not included within the suture.
The surgeon demonstrates controlled bleeding around a large pedunculated myoma without using bipolar cautery, while assistants invaginate the surrounding tissue.
The fallopian tube is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The final appearance of the sutured area is visible, showing neatly placed stitches securing the tissue.

The surgeon places the morcellator in the left iliac fossa and positions the large myoma in the pelvis, beginning the morcellation process with the device set to operate at seven hundred volts per minute.
Following morcellation, the surgeon cleans the abdominal cavity and pelvis, visually inspecting both ureters. The hole used for the morcellator is closed to reduce the risk of incisional hernia.
The final aspect of the surgery is visible, showing the completed suturing and controlled bleeding around the pedunculated myoma, with no signs of active bleeding.
The surgical field is observed with no active bleeding present, indicating successful hemostasis following the procedure.
The surgeon inspects the pelvis, confirming that no pieces of myoma remain visible after the morcellation process.
The surgical scar appears completely bloodless, indicating successful hemostasis following the procedure.

The surgeon positions the clip in place, revealing the ureteral rotation. The superior vesicle artery is visible anteriorly to the uterine artery, and the ureter is clearly seen with good rotation. The left side is controlled.
